candidates.direction_name: Trim PTX Wait/Sync Handoff On The 128x128 Anchor
ranking_notes: This is the most direct response to the cuBLAS comparison: our gap is dominated by synchronization quality, not by lack of raw occupancy.
It also aligns best with the human guidance on Async Copy, Pg2s, Ps2r, and Stage because it improves the existing staged pipeline instead of widening it.
Round 5's flat 256x128 transplant result is the reason this family moves ahead of both the non-PTX occupancy probe and the broader 256x128 reuse branch.
candidates.hypothesis: The current 256x128 branch is no longer the right place to keep pushing: it already reached 167 regs/thread and 16.66% active warps, but it still lost badly because the synchronization regime stayed at 8.32% barrier stall and the overall kernel remained far from the cuBLASLt ceiling. cuBLAS reaches 49.39% tensor activity with only 8.33% active warps because it keeps barrier and scoreboard cost near zero. That makes the next coherent move a return to the tighter 128x128 PTX anchor plus a narrow handoff surgery that trims the wait-group and export cadence while keeping the smaller 22,016 B shared-memory footprint.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Barrier cadence and export/control handoff inside the single-K 128x128 PTX microkernel, especially the seam between finishing a tile, releasing the stage with __syncthreads(), and refilling the reused buffer.
candidates.risk: Moderate-high. This stays inside a bounded hot-band surface, but it is still PTX control-path surgery and therefore sensitive to both correctness and codegen shifts.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base and the 22.000000 ms cuBLAS baseline
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the edit increases shared memory beyond the 22,016 B-class 128x128 anchor footprint, or if barrier and long-scoreboard metrics stay flat while runtime remains above the accepted base.
expected_local_changes: Restore the 128x128 PTX anchor launch path from the current losing 256x128 pivot.
Retune the PTX wait/sync or export batching seam without reintroducing the larger x32-style shared-memory footprint.

candidates.direction_name: Force 3-CTA Residency On The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
ranking_notes: This remains the cleanest restore-plus-probe direction in the queue because it stays closest to the accepted base and only nudges one machine-state axis.
It ranks behind dir_01 because the cuBLAS profile shows that pure occupancy is not the main missing ingredient on this workload.
If dir_01 stalls, this is the best next fallback for a fast bounded test on top of the accepted 128x128 family.
candidates.hypothesis: The current source should still be pulled back toward the accepted 128x128 sibling before making another broad bet. A bounded 3-CTA experiment on that sibling remains worth keeping because it starts from the already-proven non-PTX 128x128 surface that produced the current accepted base at 24.195072 ms. If the sibling can lower its register budget enough to gain latency hiding without reproducing the PTX microkernel's barrier pathology, it is the cleanest alternate family still capable of improving the accepted base with a small edit.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and latency hiding on the accepted non-PTX 128x128 hot-band surface.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The surface is correctness-safe and the edit is small, but cuBLAS makes it clear that more active warps alone will not close the remaining gap if synchronization degrades.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if registers stay near the accepted-base level, or if active warps rise but barrier cost regresses into another losing occupancy probe.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted non-PTX 128x128 sibling launch path.
Raise the launch-bounds target on that sibling to test whether 3-CTA residency is viable without repeating the PTX-specific sync tax.

candidates.direction_name: Reopen The 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Branch Later
ranking_notes: Keep this family alive because it is still the one most directly aligned with the user's 256x128 tiling guidance.
Demote it to third because the latest two clean-loop measurements do not justify another immediate 256x128 implementation before recovering the faster 128x128 surface.
If the next 128x128 recovery round still plateaus, this remains the obvious high-ceiling family to reopen with a broader repair.
candidates.hypothesis: The user guidance still points toward a 256x128 / 64x64 tiling family, and the 256x128 branch is still the only place in repo history with a plausible path to a much lower register budget. But the latest two measurements say the live correctness-safe 256x128 surface is not ready yet: the simple pivot promotion regressed by almost 6 ms, and the bounded compact transplant changed essentially nothing. That means the right move this round is to keep the family open in the queue while deferring further work until the loop has recovered onto a better 128x128 surface.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register reuse, B-fragment lifetime, and writer-ownership constraints inside the 256x128 hot-band pivot.
candidates.risk: High. This is still the broadest active family and it already has one strong regression plus one flat follow-up in the current clean loop.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately on another 28-30 ms class regression or on any correctness failure; do not spend consecutive rounds on this family without a new machine-state change.
expected_local_changes: Keep the correctness-safe 256x128 writer ownership path.
Only reopen this family after the loop has recovered to a faster 128x128 surface and can afford a broader register-reuse repair.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
